cards.front: Compare autonomous AP and controller-based (lightweight) AP deployment models.
cards.back: Autonomous: each AP is a complete device, configured individually (CLI/web). Doesn't scale — every AP its own SSID, security, etc. Controller-based: APs are 'lightweight' — minimal local config, all policy/intelligence on a central WLC. Scales to hundreds of APs with consistent config and seamless roaming.

cards.front: What is CAPWAP, and what 2 separate tunnels does it use?
cards.back: Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points (CAPWAP) — IETF protocol between Lightweight APs and the WLC. Two UDP tunnels: Control on UDP 5246 (encrypted by default with DTLS), Data on UDP 5247 (NOT encrypted by default, can be enabled). Replaced Cisco's older LWAPP. AP→WLC discovery uses methods like CAPWAP broadcast, DHCP option 43, DNS, or static.

Split-MAC (lightweight + WLC, default Cisco): real-time 802.11 functions (acks, retransmissions, beacons) handled by AP; non-real-time (auth, association, encryption keying, mgmt) handled by WLC. Local-MAC: AP handles everything, WLC just sets policy. Split-MAC enables centralized control.

cards.front: What is FlexConnect (formerly H-REAP), and when is it useful?
cards.back: Lightweight AP mode where the AP can switch user data LOCALLY (instead of tunneling to the WLC) when WAN to the WLC is slow. Useful in branch offices: APs report to a centralized WLC over WAN, but data stays on local LAN. Maintains operation if WAN to WLC fails.

cards.front: What's the difference between embedded WLC, cloud-based, and on-prem WLC?
cards.back: Embedded WLC: WLC software on the switch (like Cisco Catalyst 9800). On-prem hardware WLC: dedicated appliance (e.g., 9800-CL VM or 5520 hardware). Cloud-based: Cisco Meraki MR APs managed from cloud dashboard — no on-site WLC needed. Cloud is simplest; embedded saves rack space; on-prem is most powerful.

cards.front: What's an SSID-to-VLAN mapping, and why does it matter?
cards.back: Each SSID is mapped to a single VLAN at the WLC. Wireless clients on that SSID get DHCP from that VLAN's pool, and traffic is tagged onto that VLAN at the AP-to-switch trunk. Used to separate guest, employee, IoT into different VLANs/subnets/security policies.

cards.front: Three 802.11 frame TYPES, with examples of frames in each.
cards.back: Management: Beacon, Probe Request/Response, Authentication, Association Request/Response, Reassociation, Disassociation, Deauth. Control: RTS, CTS, ACK, Block ACK, PS-Poll. Data: actual user payload + QoS Data. The Type+Subtype bits in the Frame Control field determine which.

cards.front: Active vs passive 802.11 scanning — how does each work?
cards.back: Active scan: client sends a Probe Request on each channel; APs reply with Probe Response (containing SSID, supported rates, capabilities). Faster. Passive scan: client just listens on each channel for Beacons (sent ~10/sec by APs). Slower but draws no transmissions. Hidden SSIDs aren't found by passive scanning unless the client probes for the exact SSID.

1) Not Authenticated, not Associated. 2) Authenticated, not Associated. 3) Authenticated AND Associated (fully connected — can send/receive data). Authentication is the older 802.11 step (often 'open'); the real security is via 802.1X/EAP/WPA2-3 after association.

cards.front: Lightweight AP operating modes: Local, FlexConnect, Sniffer, Monitor, Rogue Detector, SE-Connect, Bridge/Mesh, Flex+Bridge — what is each?
cards.back: Local (default): serves a BSS to clients. FlexConnect: also serves clients but can switch traffic locally if the WLC tunnel is down. Sniffer: captures 802.11 frames and forwards to a remote analyzer. Monitor: listens passively to detect rogue devices. Rogue Detector: listens on the WIRED network for rogue MACs. SE-Connect: dedicated RF spectrum analysis on all channels. Bridge/Mesh: forms point-to-point or mesh wireless backhaul. Flex+Bridge: combines FlexConnect with Bridge functionality.

cards.front: Autonomous vs Lightweight AP switch-port type?
cards.back: Autonomous APs connect via TRUNK ports (so they can carry multiple SSIDs/VLANs themselves). Lightweight APs connect via ACCESS ports (a single 'AP-management' VLAN — all client traffic is tunneled to the WLC inside CAPWAP). Cloud-based APs (Meraki) are also access ports.
